commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=a125b3f9fbf93982f701a4f44d0449d9aece9760)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes recurring cron error scheduling to retry retryable failures on backoff before the next natural cron slot and adds regression tests for the MiniMax 503 daily-cron case.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main can pick the next daily natural cron slot over a short retry backoff, and the PR diff shows the remaining natural-before-backoff regression without needing a live provider run.
commands.review_followup: Contributor action is needed for real behavior proof, and the branch needs a maintainer-visible correction to the retry timing before merge.

target.title: fix(cron): retry transient errors before next natural cron slot (#85888)
target.body:
## Summary

- Recurring cron error path used `Math.max(naturalNext, backoffNext)` for the next scheduled run. For daily schedules (e.g. `0 5 * * *`), `naturalNext` is ~24h ahead and always dominated the short backoff, so retries for transient provider errors were effectively suppressed.
- This matches the symptom in #85888: scheduled jobs hitting MiniMax `overloaded_error` (503) during 05:00-07:30 Asia/Shanghai never re-fired the same day, while manual reruns at 08:30+ (after the busy window) succeeded because they bypassed the broken scheduler path.
- While retry budget is available and the error is classified retryable via `resolveCronExecutionRetryHint`, schedule `min(naturalNext, backoffNext)`. Once `consecutiveErrors > retryConfig.maxAttempts` or the error is permanent, fall back to `naturalNext` through the existing `resolveCronNextRunWithLowerBound` guard. When croner cannot resolve the natural next slot for a cron schedule, the retry path also clears `nextRunAtMs` to preserve the unresolved-next-run guard from #66019.

Closes #85888

## Files

- `src/cron/service/timer.ts` — split recurring error path into "within retry budget" (use `min(naturalNext, backoffNext)`, preserve unresolved-next-run guard) vs. "exhausted / permanent" (use natural slot via existing lower-bound resolver).
- `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` — three regression tests: (1) backoff retry within same day on retryable daily-cron error, (2) clears schedule when next-run is unresolved during retry, (3) fallback to natural slot when retry budget exhausted.

## Real behavior proof

I am an external contributor and do not have a live OpenClaw + MiniMax setup to capture redacted runtime cron logs. The regression tests in `timer.regression.test.ts` exercise the exact `applyJobResult` failure path with the same `FailoverError: ... overloaded_error ... status=503` error text reported in the issue against a daily `0 5 * * *` schedule in `Asia/Shanghai`, and assert the next-run timestamp now lands within the same day instead of being pushed to the next natural slot. Source-level reasoning for the original misbehavior is documented in #85888's clawsweeper comment (timer.ts:1008 used `Math.max`, which always picked the ~24h-away naturalNext over the few-minute backoff for daily crons).

Requesting maintainer review for a `proof:` override or guidance on capturing acceptable runtime evidence.

## Test plan

- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts`
- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/retry-hint.test.ts`
- [ ] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts`
- [ ] CI green on cron suite

## Review responses

- **ClawSweeper P1 (`timer.ts:1016-1019` unresolved-next-run guard):** Addressed in the second commit. When retry budget is available but croner returns `undefined` for `naturalNext` on a cron schedule, the retry path now clears `nextRunAtMs` and logs the same `cron: next run unresolved; clearing schedule to avoid a refire loop` warning instead of firing on bare backoff. Regression added.
target.author: yetval

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
